params.text:
From receiving to responding

Beyond the first conversation, there is a clear process every complaint should follow so that nothing is lost and the person is treated fairly.

  • Acknowledge the complaint promptly — under the 2009 Regulations this must be within three working days of receiving it.
  • Record it accurately, capturing what the person said and what they would like to happen.
  • Investigate fairly — look at the facts openly and without taking sides.
  • Agree timescales — give a realistic idea of how long it will take and set an expected date for the final response.
  • Keep the person informed throughout, so they are never left wondering.
  • Respond clearly, explaining what was found, what will change and how to take it further if they are still unhappy.

Timescales that keep trust

The 2009 Regulations do not set one fixed deadline for the final response; instead they require the service to agree a realistic timescale with the complainant and keep them updated. A person also generally has up to 12 months from the event (or from when they became aware of it) to make a complaint. What matters most is that people are never left in silence — regular, honest updates are what keep trust alive.

params.question: Under the 2009 Complaints Regulations, how quickly must a complaint normally be acknowledged?

answers.text: Within three working days of receiving it.

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Correct. Acknowledgement should be made no later than three working days after the complaint is received.
No. Three months is far too long; acknowledgement is expected within three working days.

answers.text: Only once the investigation is fully complete.

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: No. Acknowledgement comes first and quickly; the investigation follows.

answers.text: There is no need to acknowledge a complaint at all.

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: No. Prompt acknowledgement is a clear requirement and shows the person they have been heard.
